title: Serotonin Syndrome
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Psychiatry, Toxicology
objectives: Recognize clinical triad of serotonin syndrome
Differentiate from NMS and other hyperthermic syndromes
Manage with supportive care and cyproheptadine

vignette.text:
A 34-year-old woman on sertraline presents after starting tramadol for back pain 2 days ago. She reports agitation, diarrhea, and feeling jittery. Her husband notes she's been confused and sweating.

Past Medical History: Depression, chronic back pain

Medications: Sertraline 100mg daily, tramadol 50mg TID (new)

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.neuro: Confused, tremulous, hyperreflexia in lower extremities, bilateral ankle clonus (>3 beats), dilated pupils

questions.stem: This patient has agitation, hyperthermia, hyperreflexia, and clonus after adding tramadol to sertraline.
questions.question: What is the clinical triad of serotonin syndrome?

options.text: SEROTONIN SYNDROME TRIAD: (1) NEUROMUSCULAR: Hyperreflexia (especially lower extremities); CLONUS (inducible, spontaneous, or ocular); Tremor; Myoclonus; Rigidity (usually not lead-pipe like NMS); (2) AUTONOMIC: Hyperthermia; Diaphoresis; Tachycardia, hypertension; Mydriasis (dilated pupils); Diarrhea; Flushing; (3) MENTAL STATUS: Agitation; Confusion; Anxiety; Hypomania; KEY FEATURES vs NMS: RAPID onset (within 24h of medication change); CLONUS and hyperreflexia (NMS has rigidity + hyporeflexia); GI symptoms (diarrhea); Mydriasis; Resolves within 24-72h of stopping offending agent

questions.explanation: Serotonin Syndrome Triad: NEUROMUSCULAR (hyperreflexia, CLONUS - key finding, tremor, myoclonus), AUTONOMIC (hyperthermia, diaphoresis, tachycardia, mydriasis, diarrhea), MENTAL STATUS (agitation, confusion). KEY vs NMS: RAPID onset (<24h), CLONUS/hyperreflexia (vs rigidity/hyporeflexia in NMS), GI symptoms, mydriasis, resolves quickly when drug stopped.

sources.text: StatPearls: Serotonin Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: She's on sertraline and recently started tramadol.
questions.question: What medications cause serotonin syndrome?

options.text: SEROTONERGIC MEDICATIONS: SSRIs: Fluoxetine, sertraline, paroxetine, citalopram; SNRIs: Venlafaxine, duloxetine; MAOIs: Phenelzine, tranylcypromine, selegiline; TCAs: Clomipramine, imipramine; OPIOIDS: Tramadol, meperidine, fentanyl, methadone; OTHERS: Trazodone, buspirone; Lithium; Linezolid (MAOI activity); Methylene blue (MAOI activity); St. John's Wort; MDMA, amphetamines, cocaine; Triptans; Ondansetron; HIGH-RISK COMBINATIONS: MAOI + any serotonergic (most dangerous); SSRI + tramadol/meperidine; SSRI + MAOI (contraindicated); This patient: SSRI + tramadol = classic combination

questions.explanation: Serotonergic Medications: SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, TCAs, opioids (tramadol, meperidine, fentanyl), trazodone, linezolid (MAOI activity), methylene blue (MAOI), St. John's Wort, MDMA, triptans. HIGH-RISK COMBINATIONS: MAOI + any serotonergic (most dangerous), SSRI + tramadol/meperidine. This case: SSRI + tramadol is a classic combination causing serotonin syndrome.

sources.text: StatPearls: Serotonin Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to confirm the diagnosis.
questions.question: How is serotonin syndrome diagnosed?

options.text: HUNTER CRITERIA (most validated): In presence of serotonergic agent, requires ONE of: (1) Spontaneous clonus; (2) Inducible clonus + agitation or diaphoresis; (3) Ocular clonus + agitation or diaphoresis; (4) Tremor + hyperreflexia; (5) Hypertonia + temp >38°C + ocular or inducible clonus; DIAGNOSIS IS CLINICAL: No confirmatory lab test; CK may be mildly elevated (vs markedly elevated in NMS); TSH (rule out thyroid storm); Tox screen (rule out sympathomimetics); DIFFERENTIAL: NMS (slower onset, rigidity, hyporeflexia); Malignant hyperthermia (anesthesia exposure); Anticholinergic toxicity (dry, no clonus); Sympathomimetic toxicity; Thyroid storm

questions.explanation: Hunter Criteria for Serotonin Syndrome: With serotonergic agent, need ONE of: Spontaneous clonus, Inducible clonus + agitation/diaphoresis, Ocular clonus + agitation/diaphoresis, Tremor + hyperreflexia, Hypertonia + fever + clonus. CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS - no specific test. CK mildly elevated (vs markedly in NMS). Differentiate from NMS, malignant hyperthermia, anticholinergic toxicity.

sources.text: StatPearls: Serotonin Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Boyer EW, Shannon M. The serotonin syndrome. N Engl J Med. 2005;352(11):1112-1120. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: How is serotonin syndrome managed?

options.text: SEROTONIN SYNDROME MANAGEMENT: STOP OFFENDING AGENT(S): Most important step; SUPPORTIVE CARE: IV fluids; Cooling measures if hyperthermic; Benzodiazepines for agitation/rigidity/seizures; Cardiac monitoring; CYPROHEPTADINE (serotonin antagonist): For moderate-severe cases; Loading: 12mg PO/NG; Then: 2mg q2h until response; Max: 32mg/24h; Cannot give IV - only PO/NG; AVOID: Antipyretics (won't work - hyperthermia is from muscle activity); Restraints (worsen hyperthermia); SEVERE CASES: Intubation and paralysis if needed for temperature control; Continuous sedation; ICU admission; PROGNOSIS: Usually resolves within 24-72 hours; Mortality low with supportive care

questions.explanation: Serotonin Syndrome Management: STOP OFFENDING AGENTS (most important). SUPPORTIVE: Fluids, cooling, benzodiazepines for agitation. CYPROHEPTADINE (serotonin antagonist) for moderate-severe: 12mg load then 2mg q2h, PO/NG only. AVOID antipyretics (won't work), restraints (worsen hyperthermia). SEVERE: Intubation, paralysis, ICU. PROGNOSIS: Usually resolves 24-72h with good outcomes.

sources.text: StatPearls: Serotonin Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Boyer EW, Shannon M. The serotonin syndrome. N Engl J Med. 2005;352(11):1112-1120. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: How do you differentiate serotonin syndrome from NMS?

options.text: NMS has rapid onset like serotonin syndrome

SEROTONIN SYNDROME vs NMS: SEROTONIN SYNDROME: Onset: RAPID (<24h); Medications: Serotonergic agents; Neuromuscular: Hyperreflexia, CLONUS; Muscle tone: Increased (not lead-pipe); Pupils: MYDRIASIS (dilated); GI: Diarrhea, hyperactive bowels; CK: Mild elevation; Resolution: 24-72 hours; NMS: Onset: SLOW (days to weeks); Medications: Dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics); Neuromuscular: HYPOREFLEXIA; Muscle tone: LEAD-PIPE RIGIDITY; Pupils: Normal; GI: Decreased bowel sounds; CK: MARKEDLY elevated (>1000); Resolution: Days to weeks; Treatment: Bromocriptine, dantrolene; KEY DIFFERENTIATORS: Clonus and hyperreflexia = Serotonin syndrome; Lead-pipe rigidity and hyporeflexia = NMS

questions.explanation: Serotonin Syndrome vs NMS: KEY DIFFERENCES - SS: Rapid onset (<24h), serotonergic drugs, CLONUS/hyperreflexia, mydriasis, diarrhea, mild CK, resolves 24-72h. NMS: Slow onset (days-weeks), dopamine antagonists, LEAD-PIPE RIGIDITY/hyporeflexia, normal pupils, decreased bowels, high CK, resolves over days-weeks, treat with bromocriptine/dantrolene.

sources.text: StatPearls: Serotonin Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) RECOGNIZE: Agitation + hyperthermia + clonus + hyperreflexia after adding tramadol to SSRI; (2) APPLY HUNTER CRITERIA: Inducible clonus + agitation + diaphoresis = MEETS CRITERIA; (3) STOP TRAMADOL AND SERTRALINE immediately; (4) SUPPORTIVE CARE: IV fluids; Benzodiazepines for agitation; Cooling measures; Cardiac monitoring; (5) CYPROHEPTADINE: 12mg PO/NG load, then 2mg q2h; (6) AVOID: Antipyretics (ineffective); Physical restraints; (7) MONITORING: Temperature, mental status, neuromuscular exam; (8) EXPECTED COURSE: Should improve within 24-72h; If not improving, reconsider diagnosis; (9) DISCHARGE PLANNING: Educate on drug interactions; Alternative pain management without tramadol; May restart SSRI after resolution with close monitoring

questions.explanation: Complete Approach: RECOGNIZE triad after serotonergic drug combination. APPLY Hunter criteria - this patient meets with clonus + agitation + diaphoresis. STOP both serotonergic agents. SUPPORTIVE: Fluids, benzodiazepines, cooling, monitoring. CYPROHEPTADINE for moderate-severe (12mg load, 2mg q2h). AVOID antipyretics and restraints. EXPECT improvement 24-72h. EDUCATE on drug interactions at discharge.

sources.text: StatPearls: Serotonin Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Boyer EW, Shannon M. The serotonin syndrome. N Engl J Med. 2005;352(11):1112-1120. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected serotonin syndrome?

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in serotonin syndrome. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in serotonin syndrome. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in serotonin syndrome. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of serotonin syndrome. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

A.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: When managing serotonin syndrome, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents 2 hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of acetaminophen. Serum APAP level at 4 hours post-ingestion is 220 mcg/mL. Using the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, what is the appropriate management?

options.text: Obtain LFTs and treat only if transaminases are elevated
options.explanation: Hepatotoxicity from acetaminophen is delayed (ALT typically rises at 24-72 hours). Normal LFTs at 4 hours do not exclude impending liver injury. The nomogram guides treatment BEFORE liver damage occurs.

options.text: Recheck level at 8 hours before deciding on treatment
options.explanation: With a clearly supratherapeutic level at 4 hours, treatment should begin immediately. Delaying NAC beyond 8 hours post-ingestion significantly increases risk of hepatotoxicity.

options.text: Administer activated charcoal only — NAC is not indicated within 4 hours of ingestion
options.explanation: Activated charcoal may be beneficial if given within 1-2 hours of ingestion, but it is NOT a substitute for NAC when the level indicates toxicity. NAC should be started as soon as possible.

options.text: Start IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — the level is above the treatment line
options.explanation: On the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, the treatment line at 4 hours is 150 mcg/mL (FDA line) or 200 mcg/mL (original Rumack-Matthew line). At 220 mcg/mL, the level exceeds both thresholds. IV NAC (150 mg/kg loading, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h) should be started immediately.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Start IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — the level is above the treatment line.
correct.deeperWhy: On the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, the treatment line at 4 hours is 150 mcg/mL (FDA line) or 200 mcg/mL (original Rumack-Matthew line). At 220 mcg/mL, the level exceeds both thresholds. IV NAC (150 mg/kg loading, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h) should be started immediately.
